text: The surgeon attempts to elevate the sigmoid mesentery, but the small bowel obstructs the view, making visualization challenging.
The surgeon initiates a lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, beginning the process of separating the mesentery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uncovers an abscess near the left pelvic side wall during the dissection, presenting a visual challenge in the surgical field.
The surgeon scores the bowel, making precise incisions along its surface.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection of the bowel, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon works down the left pelvic side wall, encountering dense adhesions that make the tissue appear tightly adhered.
The surgeon attempts to dissect distally, working to separate the left dome of the bladder from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon attempts to gain mobility by carefully dissecting and freeing adhesions along the left pelvic side wall, progressing from lateral to medial.
The surgeon applies significant traction to the tissue, attempting to bluntly separate and open the adhered structures.
The surgeon inadvertently enters a chronic abscess cavity while working in the surgical field, revealing the unexpected presence of the cavity.
The surgeon attempts to gain better access to the surgical area by approaching through the mesentery, aiming to increase mobility for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies an inadvertent window created in the mesentery and utilizes it to proceed with the dissection.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect down towards the upper rectum, navigating through the surgical field with visible tissue manipulation.
The surgeon selects the margin on the left side of the rectum and uses it to assist in separating the mesentery from the adjacent sidewall.
The surgeon uses an instrument to cut through the abscess cavity, visibly separating the infected tissue.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid inadvertently pulling the ureter or iliac vessels off the sidewall while side biting.
The surgeon skives into the rectal mesentery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon has completely cleared the upper rectum, visibly separating it from the adjacent cavity.
The surgeon uses a surgical stapler to secure the proximal bowel, ensuring closure of the bowel segment.
The surgeon identifies the proximal margin of the bowel, marking the boundary for resection.
The surgeon observes the bowel, noting that the scoring on the serosal surface was made far proximal to the current location.
The surgeon staples the distal section of the bowel, completing the transection of the bowel segment.
The surgeon prepares to perform a sewn anastomosis, aligning the bowel ends for suturing to create a continuous passage after resection.
The surgical specimen is extracted through a fan and steel incision, which is visually observed to cause minimal discomfort to the patient.
The surgeon sews the inner layer of the bowel after completing the colotomy and proctotomy.
The surgeon places the second inner layer suture, moving towards the patient's right side.
The surgeon ties two sutures together, completing the inner layer closure after the colotomy and proctotomy.
The surgeon begins the outer row of Tuo PDS sutures to imbricate the previously sewn inner layer, ensuring secure closure of the colotomy and proctotomy sites.
